nativeRange.habitat: Streams and slow-moving rivers in mainland Southeast Asia and southern China; often found in calcareous (limestone) streams with harder water than most Cryptocoryne species.

decorPreferences: Nutrient-rich substrate (root tabs strongly recommended — heavy root feeder)
Place in background where long strap leaves have vertical room (45+ cm clearance)
Tolerates harder/alkaline water unusually well for a Cryptocoryne — pairs naturally with livebearer and Rift Lake setups

summary: A tall background Cryptocoryne with distinctive long puckered/textured strap leaves. Trade-named after the Balansae form of Cryptocoryne crispatula, this is one of the most water-chemistry-tolerant crypts and is notably less prone to crypt melt than most of the genus. Suitable for hard-water and Rift Lake setups where other Cryptocoryne struggle.
careNotes:
Cryptocoryne balansae is the long-standing trade name for the broad-leaved Balansae form of Cryptocoryne crispatula. Modern taxonomy treats it as Cryptocoryne crispatula var. balansae rather than a separate species. Distinctive feature: long narrow strap leaves (up to 60+ cm) with a heavily puckered/quilted surface texture. Husbandry is typical Cryptocoryne — heavy root feeder, slow-to-medium growth, melt-during-acclimation is common but usually recovers from the rhizome — with the key exception that this species tolerates and even prefers harder, more alkaline water (pH 6.5–8.0, gH up to 25) than most Cryptocoryne. This makes it one of the few aquarium plants suitable for Rift Lake cichlid setups, alongside Vallisneria and Anubias. Background placement; needs at least 45 cm of vertical tank clearance. 

NOTE: this entry was written from genus baselines after two dispatch attempts hung on upstream sources. Reviewer should verify the harder-water tolerance vs published references (Tropica, SeriouslyFish, Crypts.de).

primary.name: Cryptocoryne wendtii sister entry (fw-plant-024)
